message:
beads sync: reopen bd-4bit6 / bd-51a78 / bd-elah2 from concurrent agent state

Three beads flipped from closed back to open / in_progress in the
shared .beads/issues.jsonl file:

- bd-4bit6 ("Mock/stub finder sweep across fs crates"): closed →
  in_progress with updated_at refreshed to 2026-04-26T16:13:19Z; close
  reason and prior assignee history dropped.
- bd-51a78 ("Fuzz harness: ioctl + splice + mount stress fuzzers"):
  closed → open, reset back to creation-time updated_at (2026-04-24
  22:22:37Z), close reason / notes / closed_at all cleared.
- bd-elah2 ("FuseInodeLocks.table grows unboundedly"): closed →
  in_progress with updated_at rolled back to 2026-04-23T22:09:57Z,
  close_reason/closed_at cleared.

These match the multi-agent JSONL-as-source-of-truth pattern: another
agent's br session decided the closures were premature and re-opened
them. Committing the JSONL flush-only export so the canonical
.beads/issues.jsonl stays in sync with the in-flight agent state and
br sync --flush-only does not regress on the next tick.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"bd-4y8f","title":"Add btrfs kernel_reference golden tests matching ext4 coverage pattern","description":"## What
Add structured golden-fixture conformance tests for btrfs metadata parsing, matching the
ext4 kernel_reference.rs pattern of: create real image → parse → compare to golden JSON.

## Prior Art (what already exists)
- ext4 has crates/ffs-harness/tests/kernel_reference.rs with multiple test variants:
  creates real mkfs.ext4 images, parses them, validates against golden JSON fixtures
- btrfs E2E tests exist in fuse_e2e.rs (lines 1889-1910) that create mkfs.btrfs images
  and mount them via FUSE
- btrfs conformance fixtures in conformance/fixtures/ are synthetic JSON (not from real images)
- ffs-core unit tests build synthetic btrfs images in-memory (build_btrfs_fsops_image)

## The Specific Gap
btrfs has no equivalent of kernel_reference.rs that:
1. Creates a real btrfs filesystem with known content
2. Parses the superblock, tree structures, and metadata with FrankenFS
3. Compares the parsed output against committed golden JSON fixtures
4. Catches regressions when parsing code changes

## Implementation Plan

### Step 1: Create crates/ffs-harness/tests/btrfs_kernel_reference.rs

fn btrfs_golden_basic_superblock()
  - mkfs.btrfs -f --nodesize 16384 on 256MB sparse file
  - Parse superblock with BtrfsSuperblock::parse_superblock_region()
  - Compare: magic, sectorsize, nodesize, total_bytes, num_devices, csum_type
  - Golden: conformance/fixtures/btrfs_basic_superblock.json

fn btrfs_golden_with_files()
  - mkfs.btrfs → mount → create 3 test files with known content → umount
  - Parse: walk root tree → fs tree → verify inode items, dir items, extent data
  - Golden: conformance/fixtures/btrfs_with_files.json

fn btrfs_golden_with_compression()
  - mkfs.btrfs → mount -o compress=zlib → create files → umount
  - Parse: verify EXTENT_DATA items have compression type set
  - Golden: conformance/fixtures/btrfs_compressed.json

fn btrfs_golden_with_subvolume()
  - mkfs.btrfs → mount → btrfs subvolume create → add files → umount
  - Parse: verify ROOT_ITEM for subvolume, walk subvol tree
  - Golden: conformance/fixtures/btrfs_subvolume.json

fn btrfs_golden_chunk_and_device_trees()
  - mkfs.btrfs → parse chunk tree entries → verify stripe mapping
  - Golden: conformance/fixtures/btrfs_chunk_tree.json

### Step 2: Create golden fixture files
- Run tests once with REGEN_GOLDENS=1 to generate initial fixtures
- Commit fixtures to conformance/fixtures/btrfs_*.json
- Subsequent runs compare against committed fixtures

### Step 3: Add regeneration support
- scripts/regen_btrfs_goldens.sh: runs tests with REGEN_GOLDENS=1
- Similar to existing scripts/verify_golden.sh for ext4

## Considerations
- mkfs.btrfs minimum image: ~128MB for single-device, use sparse files
- btrfs-progs required: mkfs.btrfs, btrfs (for subvolume create, property set)
- Tests must skip gracefully when btrfs-progs not installed
- Some features (multi-device RAID) need multiple loop devices — skip in CI,
  add as a separate optional test
- Golden format: pretty-printed JSON matching the ext4 golden style
- Mount operations need root or fusermount3 — use btrfs-progs direct manipulation
  where possible (debugfs equivalent: btrfs inspect-internal dump-tree)

## Test Plan
- Each golden test: parse real image → compare to fixture → assert exact match
- Regeneration: REGEN_GOLDENS=1 cargo test -p ffs-harness --test btrfs_kernel_reference
- Verify: changing parsing code causes golden mismatch (regression detection works)
- Skip tests gracefully when mkfs.btrfs unavailable

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] 5 btrfs golden conformance tests implemented and passing
- [ ] Golden JSON fixtures committed to conformance/fixtures/
- [ ] Tests create real mkfs.btrfs images (not synthetic)
- [ ] Regression detection verified: intentional parse change → test failure
- [ ] Tests skip gracefully when btrfs-progs unavailable
- [ ] Regeneration script works","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-06T15:49:25.746184725Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-06T23:05:11.260412024Z","closed_at":"2026-04-06T23:05:11.260211284Z","close_reason":"Completed","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

Update FEATURE_PARITY evidence.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-26T01:24:22.213974126Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-26T01:29:38.662718049Z","closed_at":"2026-04-26T01:29:38.660714105Z","close_reason":"Added mounted-path BTRFS_IOC_INO_LOOKUP parity probe covering treeid=0 root resolution, seeded workspace path lookup, runtime-created child lookup through COW metadata, and missing-object ENOENT. -{"id":"bd-elah2","title":"[MAINT] FuseInodeLocks.table grows unboundedly — every accessed inode allocates a FuseInodeLock that is never evicted","description":"# Maintainability / resource finding from modes-of-reasoning pass

## Finding

`FuseInodeLocks` at crates/ffs-fuse/src/lib.rs:703-737 stores a `Mutex<BTreeMap<InodeNumber, Arc<FuseInodeLock>>>`. Every FUSE mutation operation (create/mkdir/unlink/rmdir/rename/setattr) calls `acquire()`, which does `table.entry(ino).or_insert_with(...)` — inserting a new entry on first encounter and never evicting.

For a long-running FUSE mount that touches many inodes, the table grows monotonically. With ~80 bytes per entry (key + Arc + FuseInodeLock fields), a mount that accesses a million inodes parks ~80 MB of never-freed locks.

## Evidence

- Storage: crates/ffs-fuse/src/lib.rs:705 `table: Mutex<BTreeMap<InodeNumber, Arc<FuseInodeLock>>>`
- Insertion: line 727 `table.entry(ino).or_insert_with(|| Arc::new(FuseInodeLock::default()))`
- No `remove` call anywhere — confirmed via `rg 'table\.\(remove\|clear\|retain\)' crates/ffs-fuse/`
- No TTL, no LRU eviction, no drop-on-last-guard hook

## Why this wasn't caught earlier

Not a deadlock (inspected in /deadlock-finder pass, out of that skill's scope). Not a correctness bug. Pure resource accumulation that only matters for long-running mounts. Relevant as soon as FrankenFS targets real production workloads.

## Fix directions

1. **Drop-on-last-guard cleanup.** When `FuseInodeGuard::drop` runs, check if the `Arc<FuseInodeLock>` strong_count is 1 (we hold the last ref). If so, remove the entry from the table. Race-safe because `acquire()` holds the table mutex during insert, so we can hold it during the last-guard removal check.
2. **Epoch-based GC.** Periodically scan the table and evict entries whose `Arc` strong_count is 1 (no active guard) AND that haven't been acquired in N seconds.
3. **Cap-based eviction.** Soft cap the table size; evict idle entries LRU when the cap is exceeded.

Option 1 is cleanest and matches the natural lifecycle (entries exist only when a guard is live).

## Acceptance

- A stress test creating/unlinking 100K inodes shows FuseInodeLocks.table stays bounded (approximately proportional to concurrent guards, not to total inodes-ever-accessed).
- No regression in the sorted-ino total-order guarantee (the crate's only concurrency invariant). /deadlock-finder sweep remains CLEAN.
- Unit test: acquire+drop a single inode's guard, assert the table is empty afterward.

## Severity rationale

P3 (low) — not exploitable, not correctness, not currently a production concern (FrankenFS mounts are short-lived in V1.x). But it is a real resource leak that will matter when V2+ targets longer-running deployments. Worth tracking so it doesn't get lost.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-23T18:28:54.401068265Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-25T19:53:50.228801103Z","closed_at":"2026-04-25T19:53:50.226914009Z","close_reason":"Already shipped in commit 3b07940 (FuseInodeLocks::table eviction on last guard drop, 4 unit tests). State desync between DB close at 449aaa8 and JSONL — closing in JSONL via direct flush.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

## Finding

`FuseInodeLocks` at crates/ffs-fuse/src/lib.rs:703-737 stores a `Mutex<BTreeMap<InodeNumber, Arc<FuseInodeLock>>>`. Every FUSE mutation operation (create/mkdir/unlink/rmdir/rename/setattr) calls `acquire()`, which does `table.entry(ino).or_insert_with(...)` — inserting a new entry on first encounter and never evicting.

For a long-running FUSE mount that touches many inodes, the table grows monotonically. With ~80 bytes per entry (key + Arc + FuseInodeLock fields), a mount that accesses a million inodes parks ~80 MB of never-freed locks.

## Evidence

- Storage: crates/ffs-fuse/src/lib.rs:705 `table: Mutex<BTreeMap<InodeNumber, Arc<FuseInodeLock>>>`
- Insertion: line 727 `table.entry(ino).or_insert_with(|| Arc::new(FuseInodeLock::default()))`
- No `remove` call anywhere — confirmed via `rg 'table\.\(remove\|clear\|retain\)' crates/ffs-fuse/`
- No TTL, no LRU eviction, no drop-on-last-guard hook

## Why this wasn't caught earlier

Not a deadlock (inspected in /deadlock-finder pass, out of that skill's scope). Not a correctness bug. Pure resource accumulation that only matters for long-running mounts. Relevant as soon as FrankenFS targets real production workloads.

## Fix directions

1. **Drop-on-last-guard cleanup.** When `FuseInodeGuard::drop` runs, check if the `Arc<FuseInodeLock>` strong_count is 1 (we hold the last ref). If so, remove the entry from the table. Race-safe because `acquire()` holds the table mutex during insert, so we can hold it during the last-guard removal check.
2. **Epoch-based GC.** Periodically scan the table and evict entries whose `Arc` strong_count is 1 (no active guard) AND that haven't been acquired in N seconds.
3. **Cap-based eviction.** Soft cap the table size; evict idle entries LRU when the cap is exceeded.

Option 1 is cleanest and matches the natural lifecycle (entries exist only when a guard is live).

## Acceptance

- A stress test creating/unlinking 100K inodes shows FuseInodeLocks.table stays bounded (approximately proportional to concurrent guards, not to total inodes-ever-accessed).
- No regression in the sorted-ino total-order guarantee (the crate's only concurrency invariant). /deadlock-finder sweep remains CLEAN.
- Unit test: acquire+drop a single inode's guard, assert the table is empty afterward.

## Severity rationale

P3 (low) — not exploitable, not correctness, not currently a production concern (FrankenFS mounts are short-lived in V1.x).  {"id":"bd-z75f6","title":"Expand inode checksum metamorphic relations beyond field-content invariance","description":"The current inode-checksum metamorphic harness in crates/ffs-core/tests/metamorphic.rs (added in 785bb74) covers exactly one relation: the checksum is computed after zeroing the on-disk checksum bytes, so any prior garbage in those bytes does not affect the new checksum.

That is a useful but narrow property. The checksum function has several other behavioral contracts that protect against collision attacks and silent corruption, none of which are currently exercised:

- MR-INO: writing identical inode bytes to two different inode numbers must produce different on-disk checksums (so a per-inode forgery cannot be replayed at a different inode slot).
- MR-SEED: identical inode bytes + identical inode number with a different filesystem checksum seed must produce different checksums (so cross-filesystem tampering is detectable).
- MR-GEN: changing only the generation field flips the checksum (verifies generation participates in the digest, the standard kernel anti-replay mechanism for NFS handles).
- MR-DETECT: flipping any single bit outside the checksum field bytes must invalidate verify_inode_checksum (proves the digest covers the full inode body, not just a prefix).

Each of these maps directly onto a class of real-world ext4 vulnerabilities. Add them as proptest cases alongside the existing inode_checksum_field_content_invariance test.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"claude-frankenfs-pane","created_at":"2026-04-19T21:59:34.668414530Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-19T22:10:44.533644255Z","closed_at":"2026-04-19T22:10:44.531570110Z","close_reason":"Added MR-INO, MR-SEED, MR-GEN, MR-DETECT proptests covering inode-number sensitivity, seed sensitivity, generation sensitivity, and single-bit-flip detection. All 5 metamorphic.rs cases pass (cargo test -p ffs-core --test metamorphic), clippy clean. sha=99e8455","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"bd-z7n","title":"Track: Foundations (ffs-types + ffs-error)","description":"Define canonical newtypes, parsing primitives, and error model that every other crate depends on.\n\nAcceptance: all normative types (BlockNumber, BlockSize, InodeNumber strategy, TxnId/CommitSeq/Snapshot, ByteOffset, DeviceId, etc.) exist in ffs-types with documented invariants; ffs-error has stable variant set + errno mapping used by FUSE. All other crates use these types (no local duplicates).","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-10T03:08:25.718123153Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-10T16:01:32.112602115Z","closed_at":"2026-02-10T16:01:32.112583771Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-z7n","depends_on_id":"bd-14w","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T03:15:03.228334190Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-z7n","depends_on_id":"bd-1ds","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T03:15:03.370722205Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-z7n","depends_on_id":"bd-2oa","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T03:15:03.603178334Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-z7n","depends_on_id":"bd-3hr","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T03:15:03.526401085Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-z7n","depends_on_id":"bd-owq","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T03:15:03.446053435Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-z7n","depends_on_id":"bd-sik","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T03:15:03.299955368Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":3,"issue_id":"bd-z7n","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Why this track is first-class: filesystem bugs love unit confusion (bytes vs blocks) and inconsistent error semantics (silent truncation, wrong errno).

North star:
- ffs-types: strong newtypes + checked arithmetic so invariants are enforced at compile time.
- ffs-error: a stable user-facing error set + errno mapping for FUSE.

If this track is sloppy, every other track becomes untestable and unsafe.","created_at":"2026-02-10T03:34:38Z"}]}
 {"id":"bd-z91hc","title":"CONFORMANCE: prevent btrfs prealloc overlap over live data","description":"Queue-empty porting-to-rust fallback. btrfs fallocate(mode=0/KEEP_SIZE) currently preserves read data when the requested preallocation range overlaps an initialized extent, but the prealloc path can still insert an overlapping unwritten extent into the COW FS tree. That leaks through FIEMAP as overlapping initialized/unwritten ranges and violates the spec-first extent model. Fix the prealloc path to allocate only uncovered holes, add executable coverage, and update FEATURE_PARITY.md evidence.","notes":"Claimed by Codex for porting-to-rust queue-empty parity fallback.
